title: Ballpoint Ink on Cotton
stainChemistry: Ballpoint ink is a paste of synthetic dye (azo or triphenylmethane), wax, and mineral oil in a viscous oil carrier. The wax grips the surface; the oil wicks into cotton and drags the dye molecules in with it. Cotton is pure cellulose — tolerates IPA (isopropyl alcohol), POG, NSD, and 3% H₂O₂ at room temperature. The chemistry is sequential: dissolve the oil carrier first, then lift the dye, then oxidize any residual shadow. Water-based cleaning alone does nothing to ink — the dye is oil-borne and water beads off it.
whyThisWorks: IPA dissolves the wax and the oil carrier in one pass, releasing the dye molecules so they can transfer out of the fabric. Work from the back — tamping on a clean white cloth moves the dissolved dye OUT of the cotton, not deeper in. NSD emulsifies any residual oil film the alcohol softened. 3% H₂O₂ oxidizes the remaining dye chromophore. Hot wash finishes. Skipping the solvent and going straight to detergent leaves the ink locked in the oil carrier.
defaultAssumption: Fresh ballpoint ink (within an hour) is a high-confidence DIY on cotton. Aged ink (overnight+) adds cycles but stays in home scope. Heat-set ink (through a dryer) is pro territory.

spottingProtocol.technique: Blot what's wet, place cloth underneath
spottingProtocol.instruction: Blot any wet ink surface with a clean white towel — press, don't rub. Then place a folded white towel UNDER the stained area so the ink has somewhere to transfer to. Never work ink without a catch cloth underneath; otherwise the dissolved dye spreads sideways into clean fabric.

Apply IPA to a clean white cloth. Tamp the stain from the BACK of the fabric — the dissolved dye transfers into the under-cloth. Flip the upper cloth frequently so you're always pressing with a clean section. Never pour IPA onto cotton directly. Repeat 3–5 passes; ballpoint ink often needs multiple rounds as deeper dye layers reach the surface.
Apply NSD, work with a soft brush. NSD emulsifies the oil film the IPA softened. Rinse cool from the back.
For residual dye shadow on whites: 3% H₂O₂ drop-by-drop. Oxidizes the dye chromophore. Dwell 3–5 min, blot. One oxidizer at a time. Skip on dark or dyed cotton.
Rinse until water runs clear. Launder hot per care label. Inspect BEFORE the dryer — dryer heat sets any residual ink permanently.

steps: 1. Blot wet ink. Place a clean catch cloth UNDER the stain.
2. IPA tamp from the BACK on a white cloth. Flip cloth frequently. 3–5 passes.
3. NSD to emulsify residual oil. Cool rinse.
4. 3% H₂O₂ on residual dye shadow (whites only). 3–5 min.
5. NSD flush, hot wash per care label.
professionalProtocol.temperature: cool through spotting; hot wash post-chemistry
warnings: Solvent before water — water alone doesn't touch ink
Always tamp with cloth, never pour solvent
Always place a catch cloth UNDER the stain — prevents sideways dye spread
One oxidizer at a time; skip peroxide on dark/dyed cotton

homeSolutions.agent: Place a White Towel UNDER the Stain
homeSolutions.instruction: Before you touch the ink with anything else, fold a clean white towel and place it UNDER the stained area. When the ink dissolves in the next step, it needs somewhere to go — the catch cloth pulls it out of your garment. Skip this and the dissolved ink spreads sideways into clean fabric.

homeSolutions.agent: Rubbing Alcohol on a Clean White Cloth
homeSolutions.instruction: Dab 70%+ rubbing alcohol (drugstore isopropyl) on a clean white cloth. Press the cloth onto the stain from the BACK of the fabric, over your catch cloth. Flip the pressing cloth to a clean section every few seconds — the ink transfers onto whichever fabric is touching it. Never pour alcohol directly on the fabric. Expect 3–5 rounds as deeper layers of ink reach the surface.

If you don't have rubbing alcohol: alcohol-based hand sanitizer works the same way (it's mostly alcohol + gel). Old-school hairspray also works, but only the kind with alcohol in it — most modern hairsprays are alcohol-free and will do nothing. Read the label. Apply the same way — on a white cloth, tamp from behind, never pour.

homeSolutions.agent: Dawn + Soft Brush for the Oil
homeSolutions.instruction: Once the alcohol pass has pulled most of the ink out, work a drop of Dawn dish soap into the spot with a soft toothbrush. Dawn breaks up the oily film the ink's carrier left behind. Rinse cool.
For a faint residual shadow on white cotton: dab 3% drugstore hydrogen peroxide on the spot, let it bubble 3–5 min, blot. Skip on dark or dyed cotton — peroxide lightens fabric dye.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never mix peroxide with chlorine bleach or ammonia. And don't layer hairspray + alcohol + peroxide — one at a time with a rinse between.

homeSolutions.agent: Hot Wash, Inspect Before the Dryer
homeSolutions.instruction: Launder on the hottest setting the care label allows. Inspect BEFORE the dryer — if any ink is still visible, repeat steps 2 + 4 first. Dryer heat sets residual ink permanently.

If the ink has already been through a hot dryer or two home rounds haven't cleared it, take it to a dry cleaner. Tell them: "Ballpoint ink on cotton, age [fresh / overnight / older], home-treated with rubbing alcohol + Dawn + 3% hydrogen peroxide. [Hot-washed / not yet laundered.]"

steps: 1. Place a clean white towel UNDER the stain — gives the ink somewhere to go.
2. Rubbing alcohol on a white cloth, tamp from the BACK. Flip cloth frequently. 3–5 passes.
3. Alternatives: alcohol hand sanitizer OR alcohol-containing hairspray. Same method.
4. Dawn + soft toothbrush to lift residual oil.
5. 3% hydrogen peroxide on residual shadow (whites only).
6. Hot wash per care label. Inspect BEFORE the dryer.
7. Escalate if heat-set or two rounds failed.
products: Clean white cotton towels or stack of paper towels for the catch cloth + pressing cloth
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the ink has been through a hot dryer, two home rounds failed, or the cotton is dark dyed and peroxide isn't safe.
warnings: Solvent before water — water alone doesn't touch ballpoint ink
Never pour alcohol on fabric — tamp with cloth, catch cloth underneath
Never mix peroxide with chlorine bleach or ammonia
Inspect before the dryer — heat sets ink permanently

neverDo: Never pour alcohol on cotton — always tamp with cloth
Never skip the catch cloth under the stain — sideways dye spread
Never run ink through a dryer — heat-set ink is pro territory
Never layer oxidizers — one at a time with a flush between
fiberSensitivities: Cotton tolerates IPA, POG, NSD, 3% H₂O₂, and hot wash
Dyed cotton: IPA is safe on most dyes but test inside seam first; skip peroxide
Heat-durable once the ink is out

materialWarnings: Solvent first, water after — water alone won't touch ink
Catch cloth UNDER + tamp on TOP, always
Inspect before the dryer — heat sets ink

escalation.when: Ink has been through a hot dryer, two home rounds haven't cleared it, or dark dyed cotton where peroxide isn't safe and the alcohol-only pass plateaus.
escalation.whatToTell: "Ballpoint ink on cotton, age [fresh / overnight / older], home-treated with rubbing alcohol + Dawn + 3% H₂O₂. [Hot-washed / not yet laundered.]"

contextQuestions: How old is the stain — fresh, overnight, older?
Has it been through a hot wash or dryer?
Is the cotton white, light, or dark/dyed?
Ballpoint pen, gel pen, or permanent marker? (permanent marker is a different, harder class)
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Most home attempts fail on ink because they start with water — which beads off the oil carrier. Alcohol-first is the unlock. Catch cloth under + tamp on top is the #2 rule that separates successful home cleanup from smeared-bigger stains.
